headline: Amazon touts a 'major expansion' with OpenAI as Microsoft ties loosen
summary: Amazon announced what it called a “major expansion” of its partnership with ChatGPT maker OpenAI on Tuesday, a day after the artificial intelligence company said it was loosening its ties to longtime backer Microsoft.  OpenAI CEO Sam Altman said the collaboration with Amazon's cloud computing division, Amazon Web Services, would involve co-developing a new platform for AI agents that can do computer-based work on people's behalf.  Altman spoke via prerecorded video message to an Amazon event in San Francisco at the same time as he was appearing in federal court across San Francisco Bay in Oakland for a civil trial brought by rival OpenAI co-founder Elon Musk.

Altman spoke via prerecorded video message to an Amazon event in San Francisco at the same time as he was appearing in federal court across San Francisco Bay in Oakland for a civil trial brought by rival OpenAI co-founder Elon Musk.

headline: Oracle, CoreWeave lead AI selloff on OpenAI growth concerns
summary: STORY: Shares of AI‑linked companies slid on Tuesday after a report raised fresh questions about OpenAI’s growth prospects.The Wall Street Journal reported that OpenAI has fallen short of internal targets for new users and revenue in recent months.The report also said that OpenAI’s chief financial officer expressed concerns to other industry leaders over the company's ability to pay for future computing contracts if revenue did not accelerate.The news rippled across markets, with shares of Oracle falling as much as 6%.:: OracleThe cloud provider has reportedly signed one of the largest AI infrastructure deals ever with OpenAI — roughly three hundred billion dollars’ worth of computing power over five years.Investors have been increasingly nervous about how Oracle plans to fund its data‑center expansion, with the company’s shares, bonds and credit risk indicators also under pressure.:: CoreWeave:: AMDShares of other OpenAI partners also dropped. CoreWeave lost as much as 8%. Arm Holdings slid more than 9%. Chipmakers including Nvidia, AMD and Broadcom also declined.And shares of Japan's SoftBank Group, a major investor in OpenAI, closed down almost 10% in Tokyo trading. Despite the selloff, one investment advisor told Reuters that OpenAI's reported growth concerns do not mean that the industry is, quote, "slowing down, it just means that perhaps there's just more competition." The scrutiny comes as OpenAI lays the groundwork for a possible blockbuster IPO that could value the company as high as one trillion dollars.

raw.headline: Oracle, CoreWeave lead AI selloff on OpenAI growth concerns
raw.summary: STORY: Shares of AI‑linked companies slid on Tuesday after a report raised fresh questions about OpenAI’s growth prospects.The Wall Street Journal reported that OpenAI has fallen short of internal targets for new users and revenue in recent months.The report also said that OpenAI’s chief financial officer expressed concerns to other industry leaders over the company's ability to pay for future computing contracts if revenue did not accelerate.The news rippled across markets, with shares of Oracle falling as much as 6%.:: OracleThe cloud provider has reportedly signed one of the largest AI infrastructure deals ever with OpenAI — roughly three hundred billion dollars’ worth of computing power over five years.Investors have been increasingly nervous about how Oracle plans to fund its data‑center expansion, with the company’s shares, bonds and credit risk indicators also under pressure.:: CoreWeave:: AMDShares of other OpenAI partners also dropped. CoreWeave lost as much as 8%. Arm Holdings slid more than 9%. Chipmakers including Nvidia, AMD and Broadcom also declined.And shares of Japan's SoftBank Group, a major investor in OpenAI, closed down almost 10% in Tokyo trading. Despite the selloff, one investment advisor told Reuters that OpenAI's reported growth concerns do not mean that the industry is, quote, "slowing down, it just means that perhaps there's just more competition." The scrutiny comes as OpenAI lays the groundwork for a possible blockbuster IPO that could value the company as high as one trillion dollars.

headline: Oracle Stock Is Paying the Price for OpenAI’s Failures
summary: Oracle  stock was falling Tuesday as its relationship with ChatGPT-developer OpenAI is under scrutiny again—and taking down much of the AI trade with it even though the tech start-up has pushed back.  Its shares have fallen 15% in 2026 over concerns about its capital expenditures as it builds out artificial-intelligence data centers.  The trigger for Tuesday’s slide is a report in the The Wall Street Journal that OpenAI has recently missed its own targets for new users and revenue, leading to concerns among executives about whether it will be able to fulfill its spending commitments.

headline: OpenAI revenue concerns weigh on AI trade: Is it a buying opportunity?
summary: OpenAI (OPAI.PVT) fell short of its latest revenue and user targets, pushing investors to sell-off their shares of other AI-related companies on Tuesday morning, including Oracle (ORCL), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), and CoreWeave (CRWV). Innovator Capital Management chief investment strategist Tim Urbanowicz joins Yahoo Finance Executive Editor Brian Sozzi to examine the broader implications for the AI trade. This all comes as fellow OpenAI co-founder Elon Musk takes CEO Sam Altman to court this week over an alleged breach of the private artificial intelligence (AI) developer's non-profit mission.

headline: OpenAI Is Missing Revenue Targets—and an IPO Is Suddenly No Sure Thing
summary: The circular world of artificial intelligence investment—and the beating heart of OpenAI’s $1.4 trillion in spending commitments—was called into question on Tuesday ahead of a series of big tech earnings and renewed capex ambitions that continue to form the most important plank of the market’s record-setting April run.  The Wall Street Journal reported that OpenAI, the maker of the ChatGPT chatbot and the firm responsible for firing the starting pistol on the AI revolution in November of 2022, is missing its own revenue targets, falling short of user benchmarks, and is worried about meeting its future spending contracts if sales aren’t able to expand at a quicker pace.  The paper reported that finance chief Sarah Friar has expressed concerns for the revenue misses to colleagues and has issued companywide edicts to keep a lid on costs and put more discipline into the $852 billion business.

raw.headline: OpenAI Is Missing Revenue Targets—and an IPO Is Suddenly No Sure Thing
raw.summary: The circular world of artificial intelligence investment—and the beating heart of OpenAI’s $1.4 trillion in spending commitments—was called into question on Tuesday ahead of a series of big tech earnings and renewed capex ambitions that continue to form the most important plank of the market’s record-setting April run.  The Wall Street Journal reported that OpenAI, the maker of the ChatGPT chatbot and the firm responsible for firing the starting pistol on the AI revolution in November of 2022, is missing its own revenue targets, falling short of user benchmarks, and is worried about meeting its future spending contracts if sales aren’t able to expand at a quicker pace.  The paper reported that finance chief Sarah Friar has expressed concerns for the revenue misses to colleagues and has issued companywide edicts to keep a lid on costs and put more discipline into the $852 billion business.
